rows.english: Superior: the opposite of that because of reaping the outcome of non-delusion.

rows.pali: Suvaṇṇeti adosanissandayuttattā iṭṭhakantamanāpavaṇṇayutte.
rows.english: Fair: having a desirable, agreeable, pleasing appearance because of reaping the outcome of non-hate.

rows.pali: Dubbaṇṇeti dosanissandayuttattā aniṭṭhākantaamanāpavaṇṇayutte.
rows.english: Ugly: having undesirable, disagreeable, unpleasing appearance because of reaping the outcome of hate;

rows.pali: Anabhirūpe virūpetipi attho.
rows.english: unsightly, ill-favoured, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Sugateti sugatigate.
rows.english: Happy in their destiny: gone to a happy destiny;

rows.pali: Alobhanissandayuttattā vā aḍḍhe mahaddhane.
rows.english: or rich, very wealthy, because of reaping the outcome of non-greed.

rows.pali: Duggateti duggatigate.
rows.english: Unhappy in their destiny: gone to an unhappy destiny;

rows.pali: Lobhanissandayuttattā vā dalidde appannapāne.
rows.english: or poor with little food and drink because of reaping the outcome of greed.

rows.pali: Yathākammupageti yaṃ yaṃ kammaṃ upacitaṃ, tena tena upagate.
rows.english: 78.Faring according to their deeds: moving on in accordance with whatever deeds (kamma) may have been accumulated.

rows.pali: Tattha purimehi cavamānetiādīhi dibbacakkhukiccaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, the function of the divine eye is described by the first expressions beginning with “passing away.”

rows.pali: Iminā pana padena yathākammupagañāṇakiccaṃ.
rows.english: But the function of knowledge of faring according to deeds is described by this last expression.

rows.pali: Tassa ca ñāṇassa ayamuppattikkamo, idha bhikkhu heṭṭhā nirayābhimukhaṃ ālokaṃ vaḍḍhetvā nerayike satte passati mahādukkhamanubhavamāne.
rows.english: 79.The order in which that knowledge arises is this. Here a bhikkhu extends light downwards in the direction of hell, and he sees beings in hell undergoing great suffering.

rows.pali: Taṃ dassanaṃ dibbacakkhukiccameva.
rows.english: That vision is only the divine eye’s function.

rows.pali: So evaṃ manasikaroti "kiṃ nu kho kammaṃ katvā ime sattā etaṃ dukkhaṃ anubhavantī"ti.
rows.english: He gives it attention in this way, “After doing what deeds do these beings undergo this suffering?

rows.pali: Athassa idaṃ nāma katvāti taṃkammārammaṇaṃ ñāṇaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: ” Then knowledge that has those deeds as its object arises in him in this way, “It was after doing this.”

rows.pali: Tathā uparidevalokābhimukhaṃ ālokaṃ vaḍḍhetvā nandanavanamissakavanaphārusakavanādīsu satte passati mahāsampattiṃ anubhavamāne.
rows.english: Likewise he extends light upwards in the direction of the [sensual-sphere] divine world, and he sees beings in the Nandana Grove, the Missaka Grove, the Phārusaka Grove, etc., enjoying great good fortune.

rows.pali: Tampi dassanaṃ dibbacakkhukiccameva.
rows.english: That vision also is only the divine eye’s function.

rows.pali: So evaṃ manasikaroti "kiṃ nu kho kammaṃ katvā ime sattā etaṃ sampattiṃ anubhavantī"ti.
rows.english: He gives attention to it in this way, “After doing what deeds do these beings enjoy this good fortune?

rows.pali: Athassa idaṃ nāma katvāti taṃkammārammaṇaṃ ñāṇaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: ” Then knowledge that has those deeds as its object arises in him in this way, “It was after doing this.”

rows.pali: Idaṃ yathākammupagañāṇaṃ nāma.
rows.english: This is what is called knowledge of faring according to deeds.

rows.pali: Imassa visuṃ parikammaṃ nāma natthi, yathā cimassa, evaṃ anāgataṃsañāṇassāpi.
rows.english: 80.There is no special preliminary work for this. And as in this case, so too in the case of knowledge of the future;

rows.pali: Dibbacakkhupādakāneva hi imāni dibbacakkhunā saheva ijjhanti.
rows.english: for these have the divine eye as their basis and their success is dependent on that of the divine eye.

rows.pali: Kāyaduccaritenātiādīsu duṭṭhu caritaṃ, duṭṭhaṃ vā caritaṃ kilesapūtikattāti duccaritaṃ.
rows.english: 81.As to ill-conducted in body, etc., it is bad conduct (duṭṭhu caritaṃ), or it is corrupted conduct (duṭṭhaṃ caritaṃ) because it is rotten with defilements, thus it is ill-conduct (duccarita).

rows.pali: Kāyena duccaritaṃ, kāyato vā uppannaṃ duccaritanti kāyaduccaritaṃ, itaresupi eseva nayo.
rows.english: The ill-conduct comes about by means of the body, or the ill-conduct has arisen due to the body, thus it is ill-conduct in body; so too with the rest.

rows.pali: Samannāgatāti samaṅgībhūtā.
rows.english: Ill-conducted is endowed with ill-conduct.

rows.pali: Ariyānaṃ upavādakāti buddhapaccekabuddhasāvakānaṃ ariyānaṃ antamaso gihisotāpannānampi anatthakāmā hutvā antimavatthunā vā guṇaparidhaṃsanena vā upavādakā akkosakā garahakāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 82.Revilers of Noble Ones: being desirous of harm for Noble Ones consisting of Buddhas, Paccekabuddhas, and disciples, and also of householders who are stream-enterers, they revile them with the worst accusations or with denial of their special qualities (see Ud 44 and MN 12); they abuse and upbraid them, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Tattha natthi imesaṃ samaṇadhammo, assamaṇā eteti vadanto antimavatthunā upavadati.
rows.english: 83. Herein, it should be understood that when they say, “They have no asceticism, they are not ascetics,” they revile them with the worst accusation;

rows.pali: Natthi imesaṃ jhānaṃ vā vimokkho vā maggo vā phalaṃ vātiādīni vadanto guṇaparidhaṃsanavasena upavadatīti veditabbo.
rows.english: and when they say, “They have no jhāna or liberation or path of fruition, etc.,” they revile them with denial of their special qualities.

rows.pali: So ca jānaṃ vā upavadeyya ajānaṃ vā, ubhayathāpi ariyūpavādova hoti.
rows.english: And whether done knowingly or unknowingly it is in either case reviling of Noble Ones;

rows.pali: Bhāriyaṃ kammaṃ ānantariyasadisaṃ saggāvaraṇañca maggāvaraṇañca, satekicchaṃ pana hoti.
rows.english: it is weighty kamma resembling that of immediate result, and it is an obstacle both to heaven and to the path. But it is remediable.

rows.pali: Tassa āvibhāvatthaṃ idaṃ vatthu veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 84.The following story should be understood in order to make this clear.

rows.pali: Aññatarasmiṃ kira gāme eko thero ca daharabhikkhu ca piṇḍāya caranti.
rows.english: An elder and a young bhikkhu, it seems, wandered for alms in a certain village.

rows.pali: Te paṭhamaghareyeva uḷuṅkamattaṃ uṇhayāguṃ labhiṃsu.
rows.english: At the first house they got only a spoonful of hot gruel.

rows.pali: Therassa ca kucchivāto rujjhati.
rows.english: The elder’s stomach was paining him with wind.

rows.pali: So cintesi "ayaṃ yāgu mayhaṃ sappāyā, yāva na sītalā hoti, tāva naṃ pivāmī"ti.
rows.english: He thought, “This gruel is good for me; I shall drink it before it gets cold.”

rows.pali: So manussehi ummāratthāya āhaṭe dārukhaṇḍe nisīditvā pivi.
rows.english: People brought a wooden stool to the doorstep, and he sat down and drank it.

rows.pali: Itaro taṃ jigucchanto "atikhuddābhibhūto mahallako, amhākaṃ lajjitabbakaṃ akāsī"ti āha.
rows.english: The other was disgusted and remarked, “The old man has let his hunger get the better of him and has done what he should be ashamed to do.”

rows.pali: Thero gāme caritvā vihāraṃ gantvā daharabhikkhuṃ āha "atthi te, āvuso, imasmiṃ sāsane patiṭṭhā"ti?
rows.english: The elder wandered for alms, and on returning to the monastery he asked the young bhikkhu, “Have you any footing in this Dispensation, friend?

rows.pali: Āma, bhante, sotāpanno ahanti.
rows.english: ”— “Yes, venerable sir, I am a stream-enterer.”

rows.pali: Tena hāvuso, uparimaggatthāya vāyāmaṃ mā akāsi.
rows.english: —“Then, friend, do not try for the higher paths;

rows.pali: Khīṇāsavo tayā upavaditoti.
rows.english: one whose cankers are destroyed has been reviled by you.”

rows.pali: So taṃ khamāpesi.
rows.english: The young bhikkhu asked for the elder’s forgiveness

rows.pali: Tenassa taṃ kammaṃ pākatikaṃ ahosi.
rows.english: and was thereby restored to his former state.

rows.pali: Tasmā yo aññopi ariyaṃ upavadati, tena gantvā sace attanā vuḍḍhataro hoti, ukkuṭikaṃ nisīditvā "ahaṃ āyasmantaṃ idañcidañca avacaṃ, taṃ me khamāhī"ti khamāpetabbo.
rows.english: 85.So one who reviles a Noble One, even if he is one himself, should go to him; if he himself is senior, [426] he should sit down in the squatting position and get his forgiveness in this way, “I have said such and such to the venerable one; may he forgive me.”

rows.pali: Sace navakataro hoti, vanditvā ukkuṭikaṃ nisīditvā añjaliṃ paggahetvā "ahaṃ, bhante, tumhe idañcidañca avacaṃ, taṃ me khamathā"ti khamāpetabbo.
rows.english: If he himself is junior, he should pay homage, and sitting in the squatting position and holding out his hand palms together, he should get his forgiveness in this way, “I have said such and such to you, venerable sir; forgive me.”

rows.pali: Sace disāpakkanto hoti, sayaṃ vā gantvā saddhivihārikādike vā pesetvā khamāpetabbo.
rows.english: If the other has gone away, he should get his forgiveness either by going to him himself or by sending someone such as a co-resident.

rows.pali: Sace ca nāpi gantuṃ, na pesetuṃ sakkā hoti, ye tasmiṃ vihāre bhikkhū vasanti, tesaṃ santikaṃ gantvā sace navakatarā honti, ukkuṭikaṃ nisīditvā, sace vuḍḍhatarā, vuḍḍhe vuttanayeneva paṭipajjitvā "ahaṃ, bhante, asukaṃ nāma āyasmantaṃ idañcidañca avacaṃ, khamatu me so āyasmā"ti vatvā khamāpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 86.If he can neither go nor send, he should go to the bhikkhus who live in that monastery, and, sitting down in the squatting position if they are junior, or acting in the way already described if they are senior, he should get forgiveness by saying, “Venerable sirs, I have said such and such to the venerable one named so and so; may that venerable one forgive me.”

rows.pali: Sammukhā akhamantepi etadeva kattabbaṃ.
rows.english: And this should also be done when he fails to get forgiveness in his presence.

rows.pali: Sace ekacārikabhikkhu hoti, nevassa vasanaṭṭhānaṃ, na gataṭṭhānaṃ paññāyati, ekassa paṇḍitassa bhikkhuno santikaṃ gantvā "ahaṃ, bhante, asukaṃ nāma āyasmantaṃ idañcidañca avacaṃ, taṃ me anussarato vippaṭisāro hoti, kiṃ karomī"ti vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: 87.If it is a bhikkhu who wanders alone and it cannot be discovered where he is living or where he has gone, he should go to a wise bhikkhu and say, “Venerable sir, I have said such and such to the venerable one named so and so. When I remember it, I am remorseful. What shall I do? ”

rows.pali: So vakkhati "tumhe mā cintayittha, thero tumhākaṃ khamati, cittaṃ vūpasamethā"ti.
rows.english: He should be told, “Think no more about it; the elder forgives you. Set your mind at rest.”

rows.pali: Tenāpi ariyassa gatadisābhimukhena añjaliṃ paggahetvā khamatūti vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: Then he should extend his hands palms together in the direction taken by the Noble One and say, “Forgive me.”

rows.pali: Sace so parinibbuto hoti, parinibbutamañcaṭṭhānaṃ gantvā yāvasivathikaṃ gantvāpi khamāpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 88.If the Noble One has attained the final Nibbāna, he should go to the place where the bed is, on which he attained the final Nibbāna, and should go as far as the charnel ground to ask forgiveness.

rows.pali: Evaṃ kate neva saggāvaraṇaṃ, na maggāvaraṇaṃ hoti, pākatikameva hotīti.
rows.english: When this has been done, there is no obstruction either to heaven or to the path. He becomes as he was before.

rows.pali: Micchādiṭṭhikāti viparītadassanā.
rows.english: 89. Wrong in their views: having distorted vision.

rows.pali: Micchādiṭṭhikammasamādānāti micchādiṭṭhivasena samādinnanānāvidhakammā, ye ca micchādiṭṭhimūlakesu kāyakammādīsu aññepi samādapenti.
rows.english: Acquirers of kamma due to wrong view: those who have kamma of the various kinds acquired through wrong view, and also those who incite others to bodily kamma, etc., rooted in wrong view.

rows.pali: Ettha ca vacīduccaritaggahaṇeneva ariyūpavāde manoduccaritaggahaṇena ca micchādiṭṭhiyā saṅgahitāyapi imesaṃ dvinnaṃ puna vacanaṃ mahāsāvajjabhāvadassanatthanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: And here, though reviling of Noble Ones has already been included by the mention of verbal misconduct, and though wrong view has already been included by the mention of mental misconduct, it may be understood, nevertheless, that the two are mentioned again in order to emphasize their great reprehensibility.

rows.pali: Mahāsāvajjo hi ariyūpavādo, ānantariyasadisattā.
rows.english: 90.Reviling Noble Ones is greatly reprehensible because of its resemblance to kamma with immediate result.

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ "seyyathāpi, sāriputta, bhikkhu sīlasampanno samādhisampanno paññāsampanno diṭṭheva dhamme aññaṃ ārādheyya, evaṃsampadamidaṃ, sāriputta, vadāmi taṃ vācaṃ appahāya taṃ cittaṃ appahāya taṃ diṭṭhiṃ appaṭinissajjitvā yathābhataṃ nikkhitto, evaṃ niraye"ti (ma. ni. 1.149).
rows.english: For this is said: “Sāriputta, just as a bhikkhu possessing virtuous conduct, concentration and understanding could here and now attain final knowledge, so it is in this case, I say; if he does not abandon such talk and such thoughts and renounce such views, he will find himself in hell as surely as if he had been carried off and put there” (M I 71).18 [427]

rows.pali: Micchādiṭṭhito ca mahāsāvajjataraṃ nāma aññaṃ natthi.
rows.english: And there is nothing more reprehensible than wrong view,

rows.pali: Yathāha "nāhaṃ, bhikkhave, aññaṃ ekadhammampi samanupassāmi, yaṃ evaṃ mahāsāvajjaṃ, yathayidaṃ, bhikkhave, micchādiṭṭhi.
rows.english: according as it is said: “Bhikkhus, I do not see any one thing so reprehensible as wrong view” (A I 33).

rows.pali: Micchādiṭṭhiparamāni, bhikkhave, vajjānī"ti (a. ni. 1.310).
Kāyassa bhedāti upādiṇṇakkhandhapariccāgā.
rows.english: 91. On the breakup of the body: on the giving up of the clung-to aggregates.

rows.pali: Parammaraṇāti tadanantaraṃ abhinibbattikkhandhaggahaṇe.
rows.english: After death: in the taking up of the aggregates generated next after that.

rows.pali: Atha vā kāyassa bhedāti jīvitindriyassa upacchedā.
rows.english: Or alternatively, on the breakup of the body is on the interruption of the life faculty,

rows.pali: Parammaraṇāti cuticittato uddhaṃ.
rows.english: and after death is beyond the death consciousness.

rows.pali: Apāyanti evamādi sabbaṃ nirayavevacanameva.
rows.english: 92.A state of loss and the rest are all only synonyms for hell.

rows.pali: Nirayo hi saggamokkhahetubhūtā puññasammatā ayā apetattā, sukhānaṃ vā āyassa abhāvā apāyo.
rows.english: Hell is a state of loss (apāya) because it is removed (apeta) from the reason (aya)19 known as merit, which is the cause of [attaining] heaven and deliverance; or because of the absence (abhāva) of any origin (āya) of pleasures.

rows.pali: Dukkhassa gati paṭisaraṇanti duggati, dosabahulatāya vā duṭṭhena kammunā nibbattā gatīti duggati.
rows.english: The destiny (gati, going), the refuge, of suffering (dukkha) is the unhappy destiny (duggati); or the destiny (gati) produced by kamma that is corrupted (duṭṭha) by much hate (dosa) is an unhappy destiny (duggati).

rows.pali: Vivasā nipatanti ettha dukkaṭakārinoti vinipāto.
rows.english: Those who commit wrongdoings, being separated out (vivasa) fall (nipatanti) in here, thus it is perdition (vinipāta);

rows.pali: Vinassantā vā ettha patanti saṃbhijjamānaṅgapaccaṅgātipi vinipāto.
rows.english: or alternatively, when they are destroyed (vinassanto), they fall (patanti) in here, all their limbs being broken up, thus it is perdition (vinipāta).

rows.pali: Natthi ettha assādasaññito ayoti nirayo.
rows.english: There is no reason (aya) reckoned as satisfying here, thus it is hell (niraya).

rows.pali: Atha vā apāyaggahaṇena tiracchānayoniṃ dīpeti.
rows.english: 93.Or alternatively, the animal generation is indicated by the mention of states of loss;

rows.pali: Tiracchānayoni hi apāyo sugatito apetattā, na duggati mahesakkhānaṃ nāgarājādīnaṃ sambhavato.
rows.english: for the animal generation is a state of loss because it is removed from the happy destiny; but it is not an unhappy destiny because it allows the existence of royal nāgas (serpents), who are greatly honoured.

rows.pali: Duggatiggahaṇena pettivisayaṃ.
rows.english: The realm of ghosts is indicated by the mention of the unhappy destiny;

rows.pali: So hi apāyo ceva duggati ca, sugatito apetattā dukkhassa ca gatibhūtattā.
rows.english: for that is both a state of loss and an unhappy destiny because it is removed from the happy destiny and because it is the destiny of suffering;

rows.pali: Na tu vinipāto asurasadisaṃ avinipatitattā.
rows.english: but it is not perdition because it is not a state of perdition such as that of the asura demons.

rows.pali: Vinipātaggahaṇena asurakāyaṃ.
rows.english: The race of asura demons is indicated by the mention of perdition;

rows.pali: So hi yathāvuttena atthena apāyo ceva duggati ca sabbasamussayehi ca vinipatitattā vinipātoti vuccati.
rows.english: for that is both a state of loss and an unhappy destiny in the way already described, and it is called “perdition” (deprivation) from all opportunities.

rows.pali: Nirayaggahaṇena avīciādianekappakāraṃ nirayamevāti.
rows.english: Hell itself in the various aspects of Avīci, etc., is indicated by the mention of hell.

rows.pali: Upapannāti upagatā, tattha abhinibbattāti adhippāyo.
rows.english: Have … appeared: have gone to; have been reborn there, is the intention.

rows.pali: Vuttavipariyāyena sukkapakkho veditabbo.
rows.english: 94.The bright side should be understood in the opposite way.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana viseso, tattha sugatiggahaṇena manussagatipi saṅgayhati.
rows.english: But there is this difference. Here the mention of the happy destiny includes the human destiny,

rows.pali: Saggaggahaṇena devagatiyeva.
rows.english: and only the divine destiny is included by the mention of heavenly.

rows.pali: Tattha sundarā gatīti sugati.
rows.english: Herein, a good (sundara) destiny (gati) is a happy destiny (sugati).

rows.pali: Rūpādīhi visayehi suṭṭhu aggoti saggo.
rows.english: It is the very highest (suṭṭhu aggo) in such things as the objective fields comprising visible objects, etc., thus it is heavenly (sagga).

rows.pali: So sabbopi lujjanapalujjanaṭṭhena lokoti ayaṃ vacanattho.
rows.english: All that is a world (loka) in the sense of crumbling and disintegrating (lujjana-palujjana). This is the word meaning.

rows.pali: "Iti dibbena cakkhunā"tiādi sabbaṃ nigamanavacanaṃ.
rows.english: Thus with the divine eye, etc., is all a summing-up phrase;

rows.pali: Evaṃ dibbena cakkhunā - pe - passatīti ayamettha saṅkhepattho.
rows.english: the meaning here in brief is this: so with the divine eye … he sees.

rows.pali: 412.Evaṃ passitukāmena pana ādikammikena kulaputtena kasiṇārammaṇaṃ abhiññāpādakajjhānaṃ sabbākārena abhinīhārakkhamaṃ katvā "tejokasiṇaṃ, odātakasiṇaṃ, ālokakasiṇa"nti imesu tīsu kasiṇesu aññataraṃ āsannaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 95. Now, a clansman who is a beginner and wants to see in this way should make sure that the jhāna, which has a kasiṇa as its object and is the basis for direct-knowledge, is made in all ways susceptible of his guidance. Then one of these three kasiṇas, that is to say, the fire kasiṇa, white kasiṇa, [428] or light kasiṇa, should be brought to the neighbourhood [of the arising of divine-eye knowledge].

rows.pali: Upacārajjhānagocaraṃ katvā vaḍḍhetvā ṭhapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: He should make this access jhāna his resort and stop there to extend [the kasiṇa];

rows.pali: Na tattha appanā uppādetabbāti adhippāyo.
rows.english: the intention is that absorption should not be aroused here;

rows.pali: Sace hi uppādeti, pādakajjhānanissayaṃ hoti, na parikammanissayaṃ.
rows.english: for if he does induce absorption, the [kasiṇa] will become the support for basic jhāna, but not for the [direct-knowledge] preliminary work.

rows.pali: Imesu ca pana tīsu ālokakasiṇaṃyeva seṭṭhataraṃ.
rows.english: The light kasiṇa is the best of the three.

rows.pali: Tasmā taṃ vā itaresaṃ vā aññataraṃ kasiṇaniddese vuttanayena uppādetvā upacārabhūmiyaṃyeva ṭhatvā vaḍḍhetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So either that, or one of the others, should be worked up in the way stated in the Description of the Kasiṇas, and it should be stopped at the level of access and extended there.

rows.pali: Vaḍḍhanānayopi cassa tattha vuttanayeneva veditabbo.
rows.english: And the method for extending it should be understood in the way already described there too.

rows.pali: Vaḍḍhitaṭṭhānassa antoyeva rūpagataṃ passitabbaṃ.
rows.english: It is only what is visible within the area to which the kasiṇa has been extended that can be seen.

rows.pali: Rūpagataṃ passato panassa parikammassa vāro atikkamati.
rows.english: 96. However, while he is seeing what is visible, the turn of the preliminary work runs out.

rows.pali: Tato āloko antaradhāyati.
rows.english: Thereupon the light disappears.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ antarahite rūpagatampi na dissati.
rows.english: When that has disappeared, he no longer sees what is visible (cf. M III 158).

rows.pali: Athānena punappunaṃ pādakajjhānameva pavisitvā tato vuṭṭhāya āloko pharitabbo.
rows.english: Then he should again and again attain the basic jhāna, emerge and pervade with light.

rows.pali: Evaṃ anukkamena āloko thāmagato hotīti ettha āloko hotūti yattakaṃ ṭhānaṃ paricchindati, tattha āloko tiṭṭhatiyeva.
rows.english: In this way the light gradually gets consolidated till at length it remains in whatever sized area has been delimited by him in this way, “Let there be light here.”

rows.pali: Divasampi nisīditvā passato rūpadassanaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Even if he sits watching all day he can still see visible objects.

rows.pali: Rattiṃ tiṇukkāya maggapaṭipanno cettha puriso opammaṃ.
rows.english: 97.And here there is the simile of the man who set out on a journey by night with a grass torch.

rows.pali: Eko kira rattiṃ tiṇukkāya maggaṃ paṭipajji.
rows.english: Someone set out on a journey by night, it seems, with a grass torch.

rows.pali: Tassa sā tiṇukkā vijjhāyi.
rows.english: His torch stopped flaming.

rows.pali: Athassa samavisamāni na paññāyiṃsu.
rows.english: Then the even and uneven places were no more evident to him.

rows.pali: So taṃ tiṇukkaṃ bhūmiyaṃ ghaṃsitvā tiṇukkā puna ujjālesi.
rows.english: He stubbed the torch on the ground and it again blazed up.

rows.pali: Sā pajjalitvā purimālokato mahantataraṃ ālokaṃ akāsi.
rows.english: In doing so it gave more light than before.

rows.pali: Evaṃ punappunaṃ vijjhātaṃ ujjālayato kamena sūriyo uṭṭhāsi.
rows.english: As it went on dying out and flaring up again, eventually the sun rose.

rows.pali: Sūriye uṭṭhite ukkāya kammaṃ natthīti taṃ chaḍḍetvā divasampi agamāsi.
rows.english: When the sun had risen, he thought, “There is no further need of the torch,” and he threw it away and went on by daylight.

rows.pali: Tattha ukkāloko viya parikammakāle kasiṇāloko.
rows.english: 98.Herein, the kasiṇa light at the time of the preliminary work is like the light of the torch.

rows.pali: Ukkāya vijjhātāya samavisamānaṃ adassanaṃ viya rūpagataṃ passato parikammassa vārātikkamena āloke antarahite rūpagatānaṃ adassanaṃ.
rows.english: His no more seeing what is visible when the light has disappeared owing to the turn of the preliminary work running out while he is seeing what is visible is like the man’s not seeing the even and uneven places owing to the torch’s stopping flaming.

rows.pali: Ukkāya ghaṃsanaṃ viya punappunaṃ pavesanaṃ.
rows.english: His repeated attaining is like the stubbing of the torch.

rows.pali: Ukkāya purimālokato mahantatarālokakaraṇaṃ viya puna parikammaṃ karoto balavatarālokapharaṇaṃ.
rows.english: His more powerful pervasion with light by repeating the preliminary work is like the torch’s giving more light than before.

rows.pali: Sūriyuṭṭhānaṃ viya thāmagatālokassa yathāparicchedena ṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: The strong light’s remaining in as large an area as he delimits is like the sun’s rising.

rows.pali: Tiṇukkaṃ chaḍḍetvā divasampi gamanaṃ viya parittālokaṃ chaḍḍetvā thāmagatenālokena divasampi rūpadassanaṃ.
rows.english: His seeing even during a whole day what is visible in the strong light after throwing the limited light away is like the man’s going on by day after throwing the torch away.

rows.pali: Tattha yadā tassa bhikkhuno maṃsacakkhussa anāpāthagataṃ antokucchigataṃ hadayavatthunissitaṃ heṭṭhāpathavītalanissitaṃ tirokuṭṭapabbatapākāragataṃ paracakkavāḷagatanti idaṃ rūpaṃ ñāṇacakkhussa āpāthaṃ āgacchati, maṃsacakkhunā dissamānaṃ viya hoti, tadā dibbacakkhu uppannaṃ hotīti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 99.Herein, when visible objects that are not within the focus of the bhikkhu’s fleshly eye come into the focus of his eye of knowledge—that is to say, visible objects that are inside his belly, belonging to the heart basis, belonging to what is below the earth’s surface, behind walls, mountains and enclosures, or in another world-sphere—[429] and are as if seen with the fleshly eye, then it should be understood that the divine eye has arisen.

rows.pali: Tadeva cettha rūpadassanasamatthaṃ, na pubbabhāgacittāni.
rows.english: And only that is capable of seeing the visible objects here, not the preliminary-work consciousnesses.

rows.pali: Taṃ panetaṃ puthujjanassa paribandho hoti.
rows.english: 100. But this is an obstacle for an ordinary man.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: So hi yasmā yattha yattha āloko hotūti adhiṭṭhāti, taṃ taṃ pathavīsamuddapabbate vinivijjhitvāpi ekālokaṃ hoti, athassa tattha bhayānakāni yakkharakkhasādirūpāni passato bhayaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: Because wherever he determines, “Let there be light,” it becomes all light, even after penetrating through earth, sea and mountains. Then fear arises in him when he sees the fearful forms of spirits, ogres, etc., that are there,

rows.pali: Yena cittavikkhepaṃ patvā jhānavibbhantako hoti, tasmā rūpadassane appamattena bhavitabbaṃ.
rows.english: owing to which his mind is distracted and he loses his jhāna. So he needs to be careful in seeing what is visible (see M III 158).

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ dibbacakkhuno uppattikkamo.
rows.english: 101. Here is the order of arising of the divine eye:

rows.pali: Vuttappakārametaṃ rūpamārammaṇaṃ katvā manodvārāvajjane uppajjitvā niruddhe tadeva rūpaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā cattāri pañca vā javanāni uppajjantīti sabbaṃ purimanayeneva veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: when mind-door adverting, which has made its object that visible datum of the kind already described, has arisen and ceased, then, making that same visible datum the object, all should be understood in the way already described beginning, “Either four or five impulsions impel …” (§5)

rows.pali: Idhāpi pubbabhāgacittāni savitakkasavicārāni kāmāvacarāni.
rows.english: Here also the [three or four] prior consciousnesses are of the sense sphere and have applied and sustained thought.

rows.pali: Pariyosāne atthasādhakacittaṃ catutthajjhānikaṃ rūpāvacaraṃ.
rows.english: The last of these consciousnesses, which accomplishes the aim, is of the fine-material sphere belonging to the fourth jhāna.

rows.pali: Tena sahajātaṃ ñāṇaṃ sattānaṃ cutūpapāte ñāṇantipi dibbacakkhuñāṇantipi vuccatīti.
rows.english: Knowledge conascent with that is called “knowledge of the passing away and reappearance of beings” and “knowledge of the divine eye.”

rows.pali: Etāsu hi yadetaṃ cutūpapātañāṇasaṅkhātaṃ dibbacakkhu, tassa anāgataṃsañāṇañca yathākammupagañāṇañcāti dvepi paribhaṇḍañāṇāni honti.
rows.english: 103. Among these, the divine eye, called knowledge of passing away and reappearance, has two accessory kinds of knowledge, that is to say, “knowledge of the future” and “knowledge of faring according to deeds.”

rows.pali: Iti imāni ca dve iddhividhādīni ca pañcāti satta abhiññāñāṇāni idhāgatāni.
rows.english: So these two along with the five beginning with the kinds of supernormal power make seven kinds of direct-knowledge given here.

rows.pali: Idāni tesaṃ ārammaṇavibhāge asammohatthaṃ –
rows.english: 104. Now, in order to avoid confusion about the classification of their objects:

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ dīpanā.
rows.english: 105. Here is the explanation.

rows.pali: Cattāro hi ārammaṇattikā mahesinā vuttā.
rows.english: Four object triads have been told by the Greatest of the Sages.

rows.pali: Katame cattāro?
rows.english: What four?

rows.pali: Parittārammaṇattiko, maggārammaṇattiko, atītārammaṇattiko, ajjhattārammaṇattikoti (dha. sa. tikamātikā 13, 16, 19, 21).
rows.english: The limited-object triad, the path-object triad, the past- object triad, and the internal-object triad.20

rows.pali: 414.Tattha iddhividhañāṇaṃ parittamahaggataatītānāgatapaccuppannaajjhattabahiddhārammaṇavasena sattasu ārammaṇesu pavattati.
rows.english: 106. (1) Herein, knowledge of supernormal power [430] occurs with respect to seven kinds of object, that is to say, as having a limited or exalted, a past, future or present, and an internal or external object.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Tañhi yadā kāyaṃ cittasannissitaṃ katvā adissamānena kāyena gantukāmo cittavasena kāyaṃ pariṇāmeti, mahaggatacitte samodahati samāropeti, tadā upayogaladdhaṃ ārammaṇaṃ hotīti katvā rūpakāyārammaṇato parittārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: When he wants to go with an invisible body after making the body dependent on the mind, and he converts the body to accord with the mind (XII.119), and he sets it, mounts it, on the exalted consciousness, then taking it that the [word in the] accusative case is the proper object,21 it has a limited object because its object is the material body.

rows.pali: Yadā cittaṃ kāyasannissitaṃ katvā dissamānena kāyena gantukāmo kāyavasena cittaṃ pariṇāmeti, pādakajjhānacittaṃ rūpakāye samodahati samāropeti, tadā upayogaladdhaṃ ārammaṇaṃ hotīti katvā mahaggatacittārammaṇato mahaggatārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: When he wants to go with a visible body after making the mind dependent on the body and he converts the mind to accord with the body and sets it, mounts it, on the material body, then taking it that the [word in the] accusative case is the proper object, it has an exalted object because its object is the exalted consciousness.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana tadeva cittaṃ atītaṃ niruddhaṃ ārammaṇaṃ karoti, tasmā atītārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 107. But that same consciousness takes what has passed, has ceased, as its object, therefore it has a past object.

rows.pali: Mahādhātunidhāne mahākassapattherādīnaṃ viya anāgataṃ adhiṭṭhahantānaṃ anāgatārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: In those who resolve about the future, as in the case of the Elder Mahā Kassapa in the Great Storing of the Relics, and others, it has a future object.

rows.pali: Mahākassapatthero kira mahādhātunidhānaṃ karonto "anāgate aṭṭhārasavassādhikāni dvevassasatāni ime gandhā mā sussiṃsu, pupphāni mā milāyiṃsu, dīpā mā nibbāyiṃsū"ti (dha. sa. aṭṭha. 1434) adhiṭṭhahi.
rows.english: When the Elder Mahā Kassapa was making the great relic store, it seems, he resolved thus, “During the next two hundred and eighteen years in the future let not these perfumes dry up or these flowers wither or these lamps go out,”

rows.pali: Sabbaṃ tatheva ahosi.
rows.english: and so it all happened.

rows.pali: Assaguttatthero vattaniyasenāsane bhikkhusaṅghaṃ sukkhabhattaṃ bhuñjamānaṃ disvā udakasoṇḍiṃ divase divase purebhatte dadhirasaṃ hotūti adhiṭṭhāsi.
rows.english: When the Elder Assagutta saw the Community of Bhikkhus eating dry food in the Vattaniya Lodging he resolved thus, “Let the water pool become cream of curd every day before the meal,”

rows.pali: Purebhatte gahitaṃ dadhirasaṃ hoti.
rows.english: and when the water was taken before the meal it was cream of curd;

rows.pali: Pacchābhatte pākatikaudakameva (dha. sa. aṭṭha. 1434).
rows.english: but after the meal there was only the normal water.22

rows.pali: Kāyaṃ pana cittasannissitaṃ katvā adissamānena kāyena gamanakāle paccuppannārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 108. At the time of going with an invisible body after making the body dependent on the mind it has a present object.

rows.pali: Kāyavasena cittaṃ, cittavasena vā kāyaṃ pariṇāmanakāle attano kumārakavaṇṇādinimmānakāle ca sakāyacittānaṃ ārammaṇakaraṇato ajjhattārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time of converting the mind to accord with the body, or the body to accord with the mind, and at the time of creating one’s own appearance as a boy, etc., it has an internal object because it makes one’s own body and mind its object.

rows.pali: Bahiddhā hatthiassādidassanakāle pana bahiddhārammaṇanti evaṃ tāva iddhividhañāṇassa sattasu ārammaṇesu pavatti veditabbā.
rows.english: But at the time of showing elephants, horses, etc., externally it has an external object. This is how, firstly, the kinds of supernormal power should be understood to occur with respect to the seven kinds of object.

rows.pali: 415.Dibbasotadhātuñāṇaṃ parittapaccuppannaajjhattabahiddhārammaṇavasena catūsu ārammaṇesu pavattati.
rows.english: 109.(2) Knowledge of the divine ear element occurs with respect to four kinds of object, that is to say, as having a limited, and a present, and an internal or external object.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Tañhi yasmā saddaṃ ārammaṇaṃ karoti, saddo ca paritto, tasmā parittārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Since it makes sound its object and since sound is limited (see Vibh 74), it therefore has a limited object.23 But since it occurs only by making existing sound its object, it has a present object.

rows.pali: Vijjamānaṃyeva pana saddaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā pavattanato paccuppannārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time of hearing sounds in one’s own belly it has an internal object.

rows.pali: Taṃ attano kucchisaddasavanakāle ajjhattārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of hearing the sounds of others it has an external object.

rows.pali: Paresaṃ saddasavanakāle bahiddhārammaṇanti evaṃ dibbasotadhātuñāṇassa catūsu ārammaṇesu pavatti veditabbā.
rows.english: [431] This is how the knowledge of the divine ear element should be understood to occur with respect to the four kinds of object.

rows.pali: 416.Cetopariyañāṇaṃ parittamahaggataappamāṇamaggaatītānāgatapaccuppannabahiddhārammaṇavasena aṭṭhasu ārammaṇesu pavattati.
rows.english: 110.(3) Knowledge of penetration of minds occurs with respect to eight kinds of object, that is to say, as having a limited, exalted or measureless object, path as object, and a past, future or present object, and an external object.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Tañhi paresaṃ kāmāvacaracittajānanakāle parittārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time of knowing others’ sense-sphere consciousness it has a limited object.

rows.pali: Rūpāvacaraarūpāvacaracittajānanakāle mahaggatārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time of knowing their fine-material-sphere or immaterial-sphere consciousness it has an exalted object.

rows.pali: Maggaphalajānanakāle appamāṇārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time of knowing path and fruition it has a measureless object.

rows.pali: Ettha ca puthujjano sotāpannassa cittaṃ na jānāti.
rows.english: And here an ordinary man does not know a stream- enterer’s consciousness,

rows.pali: Sotāpanno vā sakadāgāmissāti evaṃ yāva arahato netabbaṃ.
rows.english: nor does a stream-enterer know a once-returner’s, and so up to the Arahant’s consciousness.

rows.pali: Arahā pana sabbesaṃ cittaṃ jānāti.
rows.english: But an Arahant knows the consciousness of all the others.

rows.pali: Aññopi ca uparimo heṭṭhimassāti ayaṃ viseso veditabbo.
rows.english: And each higher one knows the consciousnesses of all those below him. This is the difference to be understood.

rows.pali: Maggacittārammaṇakāle maggārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time when it has path consciousness as its object it has path as object.

rows.pali: Yadā pana atīte sattadivasabbhantare ca anāgate sattadivasabbhantare ca paresaṃ cittaṃ jānāti, tadā atītārammaṇaṃ anāgatārammaṇañca hoti.
rows.english: But when one knows another’s consciousness within the past seven days, or within the future seven days, then it has a past object and has a future object respectively.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ paccuppannārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 111. How does it have a present object?

rows.pali: Paccuppannaṃ nāma tividhaṃ – khaṇapaccuppannaṃ, santatipaccuppannaṃ, addhāpaccuppannañca.
rows.english: “Present” (paccuppanna) is of three kinds, that is to say, present by moment, present by continuity, and present by extent.

rows.pali: Tattha uppādaṭṭhitibhaṅgappattaṃ khaṇapaccuppannaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, what has reached arising (uppāda), presence (ṭhiti), and dissolution (bhaṅga) is present by moment.

rows.pali: Ekadvesantativārapariyāpannaṃ santatipaccuppannaṃ.
rows.english: What is included in one or two rounds of continuity is present by continuity.

rows.pali: Tattha andhakāre nisīditvā ālokaṭṭhānaṃ gatassa na tāva ārammaṇaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti, yāva pana taṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti, etthantare ekadvesantativārā veditabbā.
rows.english: 112. Herein, when someone goes to a well-lit place after sitting in the dark, an object is not clear at first; until it becomes clear, one or two rounds of continuity should be understood [to pass] meanwhile.

rows.pali: Ālokaṭṭhāne vicaritvā ovarakaṃ paviṭṭhassāpi na tāva sahasā rūpaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti, yāva pana taṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti, etthantare ekadvesantativārā veditabbā.
rows.english: And when he goes into an inner closet after going about in a well-lit place, a visible object is not immediately evident at first; until it becomes clear, one or two rounds of continuity should be understood [to pass] meanwhile.

rows.pali: Dūre ṭhatvā pana rajakānaṃ hatthavikāraṃ, ghaṇḍibherīākoṭanavikārañca disvāpi na tāva saddaṃ suṇāti, yāva pana taṃ suṇāti, etasmimpi antare ekadvesantativārā veditabbā.
rows.english: When he stands at a distance, although he sees the alterations (movements) of the hands of washer-men and the alterations (movements) of the striking of gongs, drums, etc., yet he does not hear the sound at first (see Ch. XIV n. 22); until he hears it, one or two rounds of continuity should be understood [to pass] meanwhile.

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāva majjhimabhāṇakā.
rows.english: This, firstly, is according to the Majjhima reciters.

rows.pali: Saṃyuttabhāṇakā pana rūpasantati arūpasantatīti dve santatiyo vatvā udakaṃ akkamitvā gatassa yāva tīre akkantaudakalekhā na vippasīdati, addhānato āgatassa yāva kāye usumabhāvo na vūpasammati, ātapā āgantvā gabbhaṃ paviṭṭhassa yāva andhakārabhāvo na vigacchati, antogabbhe kammaṭṭhānaṃ manasi karitvā divā vātapānaṃ vivaritvā olokentassa yāva akkhīnaṃ phandanabhāvo na vūpasammati, ayaṃ rūpasantati nāma.
rows.english: 113. The Saṃyutta reciters, however, say that there are two kinds of continuity, that is to say, material continuity and immaterial continuity: that a material continuity lasts as long as the [muddy] line of water touching the bank when one treads in the water takes to clear,24 as long as the heat of the body in one who has walked a certain extent takes to die down, as long as the blindness in one who has come from the sunshine into a room does not depart, as long as when, after someone has been giving attention to his meditation subject in a room and then opens the shutters by day and looks out, the dazzling in his eyes does not die down;

rows.pali: Dve tayo javanavārā arūpasantati nāmāti vatvā tadubhayampi santatipaccuppannaṃ nāmāti vadanti.
rows.english: and that an immaterial continuity consists in two or three rounds of impulsions. Both of these are [according to them] called “present by continuity.”

rows.pali: Ekabhavaparicchinnaṃ pana addhāpaccuppannaṃ nāma.
rows.english: 114. What is delimited by a single becoming (existence) is called present by extent,

rows.pali: Yaṃ sandhāya bhaddekarattasutte "yo cāvuso, mano ye ca dhammā ubhayametaṃ paccuppannaṃ, tasmiṃ ce paccuppanne chandarāgappaṭibaddhaṃ hoti viññāṇaṃ, chandarāgappaṭibaddhattā viññāṇassa tadabhinandati, tadabhinandanto paccuppannesu dhammesu saṃhīratī"ti (ma. ni. 3.284) vuttaṃ.
rows.english: with reference to which it is said in the Bhaddekaratta Sutta: “Friends, the mind and mental objects are both what is present. Consciousness is bound by desire and greed for what is present. Because consciousness is bound by desire and greed he delights in that. When he delights in that, then he is vanquished with respect to present states” (M III 197).

rows.pali: Santatipaccuppannañcettha aṭṭhakathāsu āgataṃ.
rows.english: And here, “present by continuity” is used in the Commentaries

rows.pali: Addhāpaccuppannaṃ sutte.
rows.english: while “present by extent” is used in the Suttas.

rows.pali: Tattha keci khaṇapaccuppannaṃ cittaṃ cetopariyañāṇassa ārammaṇaṃ hotīti vadanti.
rows.english: 115.Herein, some25 say that consciousness “present by moment” is the object of knowledge of penetration of minds.

rows.pali: Kiṃ kāraṇā?
rows.english: What reason do they give?

rows.pali: Yasmā iddhimato ca parassa ca ekakkhaṇe cittaṃ uppajjatīti.
rows.english: It is that the consciousness of the possessor of supernormal power and that of the other arise in a single moment.

rows.pali: Idañca nesaṃ opammaṃ, yathā ākāse khitte pupphamuṭṭhimhi avassaṃ ekaṃ pupphaṃ ekassa vaṇṭena vaṇṭaṃ paṭivijjhati, evaṃ parassa cittaṃ jānissāmīti rāsivasena mahājanassa citte āvajjite avassaṃ ekassa cittaṃ ekena cittena uppādakkhaṇe vā ṭhitikkhaṇe vā bhaṅgakkhaṇe vā paṭivijjhatīti.
rows.english: Their simile is this: just as when a handful of flowers is thrown into the air, the stalk of one flower is probably struck by the stalk of another, and so too, when with the thought, “I will know another’s mind,” the mind of a multitude is adverted to as a mass, then the mind of one is probably penetrated by the mind of the other either at the moment of arising or at the moment of presence or at the moment of dissolution.

rows.pali: Taṃ pana vassasatampi vassasahassampi āvajjanto yena ca cittena āvajjati, yena ca jānāti.
rows.english: because even if one went on adverting for a hundred or a thousand years, there is never co- presence of the two consciousnesses, that is to say, of that with which he adverts and that [of impulsion] with which he knows,

rows.pali: Tesaṃ dvinnaṃ sahaṭhānābhāvato āvajjanajavanānañca aniṭṭhaṭṭhāne nānārammaṇabhāvappattidosato ayuttanti aṭṭhakathāsu paṭikkhittaṃ.
rows.english: 116. That, however, is rejected in the Commentaries as erroneous, and because the flaw of plurality of objects follows if presence [of the same object] to both adverting and impulsion is not insisted on.

rows.pali: Santatipaccuppannaṃ pana addhāpaccuppannañca ārammaṇaṃ hotīti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: What should be understood is that the object is present by continuity and present by extent.

rows.pali: Tattha yaṃ vattamānajavanavīthito atītānāgatavasena dvittijavanavīthiparimāṇe kāle parassa cittaṃ, taṃ sabbampi santatipaccuppannaṃ nāma.
rows.english: 117. Herein, another’s consciousness during a time measuring two or three cognitive series with impulsions extending before and after the [strictly] currently existing cognitive series with impulsions, is all called “present by continuity.”

rows.pali: "Addhāpaccuppannaṃ pana javanavārena dīpetabba"nti saṃyuttaṭṭhakathāyaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But in the Saṃyutta Commentary it is said that “present by extent” should be illustrated by a round of impulsions.

rows.pali: Taṃ suṭṭhu vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 118. That is rightly said.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ dīpanā, iddhimā parassa cittaṃ jānitukāmo āvajjati, āvajjanaṃ khaṇapaccuppannaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā teneva saha nirujjhati.
rows.english: Here is the illustration. The possessor of supernormal- power who wants to know another’s mind adverts. The adverting [consciousness] makes [the other’s consciousness that is] present by moment its object and ceases together with it.

rows.pali: Tato cattāri pañca vā javanāni.
rows.english: After that there are four or five impulsions,

rows.pali: Yesaṃ pacchimaṃ iddhicittaṃ, sesāni kāmāvacarāni, tesaṃ sabbesampi tadeva niruddhaṃ cittamārammaṇaṃ hoti, na ca tāni nānārammaṇāni honti, addhāvasena paccuppannārammaṇattā.
rows.english: of which the last is the supernormal-power consciousness, the rest being of the sense sphere. That same [other’s] consciousness, which has ceased, is the object of all these too, and so they do not have different objects because they have an object that is “present by extent.”

rows.pali: Ekārammaṇattepi ca iddhicittameva parassa cittaṃ jānāti, na itarāni.
rows.english: And while they have a single object it is only the supernormal-power consciousness that actually knows another’s consciousness, not the others,

rows.pali: Yathā cakkhudvāre cakkhuviññāṇameva rūpaṃ passati, na itarānīti.
rows.english: just as in the eye-door it is only eye-consciousness that actually sees the visible datum, not the others. 119.

rows.pali: Iti idaṃ santatipaccuppannassa ceva addhāpaccuppannassa ca vasena paccuppannārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: So this has a present object in what is present by continuity and what is present by extent.

rows.pali: Yasmā vā santatipaccuppannampi addhāpaccuppanneyeva patati, tasmā addhāpaccuppannavasenevetaṃ paccuppannārammaṇanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Or since what is present by continuity falls within what is present by extent, it can therefore be understood that it has a present object simply in what is present by extent.

rows.pali: Parassa cittārammaṇattāyeva pana bahiddhārammaṇaṃ hotīti evaṃ cetopariyañāṇassa aṭṭhasu ārammaṇesu pavatti veditabbā.
rows.english: It has an external object because it has only another’s mind as its object. This is how knowledge of penetration of minds should be understood to occur with respect to the eight kinds of objects.

rows.pali: 417.Pubbenivāsañāṇaṃ parittamahaggataappamāṇamaggaatītaajjhattabahiddhānavattabbārammaṇavasena aṭṭhasu ārammaṇesu pavattati.
rows.english: 120.(4) Knowledge of past lives occurs with respect to eight kinds of object, that is to say, as having a limited, exalted, or measureless object, path as object, a past object, and an internal, external, or not-so-classifiable object.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Tañhi kāmāvacarakkhandhānussaraṇakāle parittārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time of recollecting sense-sphere aggregates it has a limited object.

rows.pali: Rūpāvacarārūpāvacarakkhandhānussaraṇakāle mahaggatārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of recollecting fine-material-sphere or immaterial-sphere aggregates it has an exalted object.

rows.pali: Atīte attanā parehi vā bhāvitamaggaṃ sacchikataphalañca anussaraṇakāle appamāṇārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of recollecting a path developed, or a fruition realized, in the past either by oneself or by others, it has a measureless object.

rows.pali: Bhāvitamaggameva anussaraṇakāle maggārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of recollecting a path developed it has a path as object.

rows.pali: Niyamato panetaṃ atītārammaṇameva.
rows.english: But it invariably has a past object.

rows.pali: Tattha kiñcāpi cetopariyañāṇayathākammupagañāṇānipi atītārammaṇāni honti, atha kho tesaṃ cetopariyañāṇassa sattadivasabbhantarātītaṃ cittameva ārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: 121.Herein, although knowledge of penetration of minds and knowledge of faring according to deeds also have a past object, still, of these two, the object of the knowledge of penetration of minds is only consciousness within the past seven days.

rows.pali: Tañhi aññaṃ khandhaṃ vā khandhapaṭibaddhaṃ vā na jānāti.
rows.english: It knows neither other aggregates nor what is bound up with aggregates [that is, name, surname, and so on].

rows.pali: Maggasampayuttacittārammaṇattā pana pariyāyato maggārammaṇanti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: It is said indirectly that it has a path as object since it has the consciousness associated with the path as its object.

rows.pali: Yathākammupagañāṇassa ca atītaṃ cetanāmattameva ārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Also, the object of knowledge of faring according to deeds is simply past volition.

rows.pali: Pubbenivāsañāṇassa pana atītā khandhā khandhapaṭibaddhañca kiñci anārammaṇaṃ nāma natthi.
rows.english: But there is nothing, whether past aggregates or what is bound up with aggregates, that is not the object of knowledge of past lives;

rows.pali: Tañhi atītakkhandhakhandhapaṭibaddhesu dhammesu sabbaññutaññāṇagatikaṃ hotīti ayaṃ viseso veditabbo.
rows.english: for that is on a par with omniscient knowledge with respect to past aggregates and states bound up with aggregates. This is the difference to be understood here.

rows.pali: Ayamettha aṭṭhakathānayo.
rows.english: 122. This is the method according to the Commentaries here.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana "kusalā khandhā iddhividhañāṇassa cetopariyañāṇassa pubbenivāsānussatiñāṇassa yathākammupagañāṇassa anāgataṃsañāṇassa ārammaṇapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.404) paṭṭhāne vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But it is said in the Paṭṭhāna: “Profitable aggregates are a condition, as object condition, for knowledge of supernormal power, for knowledge of penetration of minds, for knowledge of past lives, for knowledge of faring according to deeds, and for knowledge of the future” (Paṭṭh I 154),

rows.pali: Tasmā cattāropi khandhā cetopariyañāṇayathākammupagañāṇānaṃ ārammaṇā honti.
rows.english: and therefore four aggregates are also the objects of knowledge of penetration of minds and of knowledge of faring according to deeds.

rows.pali: Tatrāpi yathākammupagañāṇassa kusalākusalā evāti.
rows.english: And there too profitable and unprofitable [aggregates are the object] of knowledge of faring according to deeds.

rows.pali: Attano khandhānussaraṇakāle panetaṃ ajjhattārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: 123. At the time of recollecting one’s own aggregates it has an internal object.

rows.pali: Parassa khandhānussaraṇakāle bahiddhārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of recollecting another’s aggregates it has an external object.

rows.pali: "Atīte vipassī bhagavā ahosi.
rows.english: “In the past there was the Blessed One Vipassin.

rows.pali: Tassa mātā bandhumatī, pitā bandhumā"tiādinā (dī. ni. 2.12) nayena nāmagottapathavīnimittādianussaraṇakāle navattabbārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: His mother was Bhandumatī. His father was Bhandumant” (see D II 6–7), - At the time of recollecting [the concepts consisting in] name, race (surname) in the way beginning (before) and [the concept consisting in] the sign of the earth, etc., it has a not-so-classifiable object.

rows.pali: Nāmagottanti cettha khandhūpanibandho sammutisiddho byañjanattho daṭṭhabbo, na byañjanaṃ.
rows.english: And here the name and race (surname, lineage) must be regarded not as the actual words but as the meaning of the words, which is established by convention and bound up with aggregates.

rows.pali: Byañjanañhi saddāyatanasaṅgahitattā parittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: For the actual words [434] are “limited” since they are included by the sound base,

rows.pali: Yathāha "niruttipaṭisambhidā parittārammaṇā"ti (vibha. 749).
rows.english: according as it is said: “The discrimination of language has a limited object” (Vibh 304).

rows.pali: Ayamettha amhākaṃ khanti.
rows.english: Our preference here is this.

rows.pali: Evaṃ pubbenivāsañāṇassa aṭṭhasu ārammaṇesu pavatti veditabbā.
rows.english: This is how the knowledge of past lives should be understood to occur with respect to the eight kinds of object.

rows.pali: 418.Dibbacakkhuñāṇaṃ parittapaccuppannaajjhattabahiddhārammaṇavasena catūsu ārammaṇesu pavattati.
rows.english: 124.(5) Knowledge of the divine eye occurs with respect to four kinds of object, that is to say, as having a limited, a present, and an internal or external object.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Tañhi yasmā rūpaṃ ārammaṇaṃ karoti, rūpañca parittaṃ, tasmā parittārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Since it makes materiality its object and materiality is limited (see Vibh 62) it therefore has a limited object.

rows.pali: Vijjamāneyeva ca rūpe pavattattā paccuppannārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Since it occurs only with respect to existing materiality it has a present object.

rows.pali: Attano kucchigatādirūpadassanakāle ajjhattārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of seeing materiality inside one’s own belly, etc., it has an internal object.

rows.pali: Parassa rūpadassanakāle bahiddhārammaṇanti evaṃ dibbacakkhuñāṇassa catūsu ārammaṇesu pavatti veditabbā.
rows.english: At the time of seeing another’s materiality it has an external object. This is how the knowledge of the divine eye should be understood to occur with respect to the four kinds of object.

rows.pali: 419.Anāgataṃsañāṇaṃ parittamahaggataappamāṇamaggaanāgataajjhattabahiddhānavattabbārammaṇavasena aṭṭhasu ārammaṇesu pavattati.
rows.english: 125.(6) Knowledge of the future occurs with respect to eight kinds of object, that is to say, as having a limited or exalted or immeasurable object, a path as object, a future object, and an internal, external, or not-so classifiable object.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Tañhi "ayaṃ anāgate kāmāvacare nibbattissatī"ti jānanakāle parittārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time of knowing this, “This one will be reborn in the future in the sense sphere,” it has a limited object.

rows.pali: "Rūpāvacare arūpāvacare vā nibbattissatī"ti jānanakāle mahaggatārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of knowing, “He will be reborn in the fine-material or immaterial sphere,” it has an exalted object.

rows.pali: "Maggaṃ bhāvessati, phalaṃ sacchikarissatī"ti jānanakāle appamāṇārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of knowing, “He will develop the path, he will realize fruition,” it has an immeasurable object.

rows.pali: "Maggaṃ bhāvessati"cceva jānanakāle maggārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of knowing, “He will develop the path,” it has a path as object too.

rows.pali: Niyamato pana taṃ anāgatārammaṇameva.
rows.english: But it invariably has a future object.

rows.pali: Tattha kiñcāpi cetopariyañāṇampi anāgatārammaṇaṃ hoti, atha kho tassa sattadivasabbhantarānāgataṃ cittameva ārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: 126. Herein, although knowledge of penetration of minds has a future object too, nevertheless its object is then only future consciousness that is within seven days;

rows.pali: Tañhi aññaṃ khandhaṃ vā khandhapaṭibaddhaṃ vā na jānāti.
rows.english: for it knows neither any other aggregate nor what is bound up with aggregates.

rows.pali: Anāgataṃsañāṇassa pubbenivāsañāṇe vuttanayena anāgate anārammaṇaṃ nāma natthi.
rows.english: But there is nothing in the future, as described under the knowledge of past lives (§121), that is not an object of knowledge of the future.

rows.pali: "Ahaṃ amutra nibbattissāmī"ti jānanakāle ajjhattārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: 127. At the time of knowing, “I shall be reborn there,” it has an internal object.

rows.pali: "Asuko amutra nibbattissatī"ti jānanakāle bahiddhārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of knowing, “So-and-so will be reborn there,” it has an external object.

rows.pali: "Anāgate metteyyo bhagavā uppajjissati (dī. ni. 3.107).
rows.english: But at the time of knowing name and race (surname) in the way beginning, “In the future the Blessed One Metteyya will arise.

rows.pali: Subrahmā nāmassa brāhmaṇo pitā bhavissati.
rows.english: His father will be the brahman Subrahmā.

rows.pali: Brahmavatī nāma brāhmaṇī mātā"tiādinā pana nayena nāmagottajānanakāle pubbenivāsañāṇe vuttanayeneva na vattabbārammaṇaṃ hotīti evaṃ anāgataṃsañāṇassa aṭṭhasu ārammaṇesu pavatti veditabbā.
rows.english: His mother will be the brahmani Brahmavatī” (see D III 76), it has a not-so-classifiable object in the way described under knowledge of past lives (§123). This is how the knowledge of the future should be understood.

rows.pali: 420.Yathākammupagañāṇaṃ parittamahaggataatītaajjhattabahiddhārammaṇavasena pañcasu ārammaṇesu pavattati.
rows.english: 128.(7) Knowledge of faring according to deeds occurs with respect to five kinds of object, that is to say, as having a limited or exalted, a past, and an internal or external object.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Tañhi kāmāvacarakammajānanakāle parittārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time of knowing sense-sphere kamma (deeds) it has a limited object.

rows.pali: Rūpāvacarārūpāvacarakammajānanakāle mahaggatārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of knowing fine-material-sphere or immaterial- sphere kamma it has an exalted object.

rows.pali: Atītameva jānātīti atītārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Since it knows only what is past it has a past object.

rows.pali: Attano kammaṃ jānanakāle ajjhattārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of knowing one’s own kamma it has an internal object.

rows.pali: Parassa kammaṃ jānanakāle bahiddhārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At the time of knowing another’s kamma it has an external object.

rows.pali: Evaṃ yathākammupagañāṇassa pañcasu ārammaṇesu pavatti veditabbā.
rows.english: This is how the knowledge of faring according to deeds should be understood to occur with respect to the five kinds of object.

rows.pali: Yañcettha ajjhattārammaṇañceva bahiddhārammaṇañcāti vuttaṃ, taṃ kālena ajjhattaṃ kālena bahiddhā jānanakāle ajjhattabahiddhārammaṇampi hotiyevāti.
rows.english: 129. And when [the knowledge] described here both as “having an internal object” and “having an external object” knows [these objects] now internally and now externally, it is then said that it has an internal-external object as well.
